summary: Gruber corrects his previous claim that Apple never advertised its Intel partnership, acknowledging the memorable 2006 'The Intel Chip' commercial he'd praised at the time. He revisits how that ad subtly framed Intel as needing the Mac to reach its potential, a classic Jobsian move. The bulk of the post critiques Intel's new 'Go PC' campaign, arguing it's delusional given Intel's current competitive position. Gruber catalogs Intel's losses across fabrication (to TSMC), mobile (to ARM), gaming CPUs (to AMD), and GPUs (to Nvidia). He argues Intel is advertising as if still atop the industry when everyone can see how far they've fallen. Rather than attacking Apple, Gruber suggests Intel needs its own 'Think Different' moment to reassert its core identity.
key_points: Gruber admits he was wrong about Apple never advertising Intel, recalling the acclaimed 2006 'The Intel Chip' commercial
The 2006 ad subtly positioned Intel as needing Apple rather than the reverse — a characteristically Jobsian framing of the partnership
Intel's current 'Go PC' campaign is out of touch because Intel can't credibly claim leadership in any major segment: fabrication, mobile, gaming CPUs, or GPUs
Intel's brand problem is confusing badge recognition with technological leadership — 'Intel Inside' meant something when they were on top
Intel needs to reassert its identity and values rather than punch at Apple; they need a 'Think Different' not a 'Go PC'
money_quotes: It paints Intel as needing the Mac for its chips to reach their potential, not the Mac as needing Intel chips to achieve performance-per-watt parity with the rest of the PC industry.
It's implicitly a bit insulting, and an utterly Jobsian way to frame the new partnership.
I stand by the claim that Apple's ad makes Intel chips look better, feel better — downright epic, even — than any ad Intel itself had ever made or has made since.
For Intel to be Intel they need to be leading the industry. The best fabs, the fastest chips. Right now they can't credibly argue that they're the best at anything.
Instead of thinking 'Intel Inside' was a badge of honor because it meant leading edge tech was powering the device, they think it's necessarily good tech inside because the Intel badge is outside.
They're advertising like they're still perched atop Relevancy Mountain when the obvious truth everyone can see is how startlingly far they've fallen from that peak.
Intel needs their version of 'Think Different'. Reassert the company's core values. Tell the world, and your own employees, how they should feel about Intel.
key_insight: Intel's 'Go PC' campaign reveals a company confusing brand nostalgia with market reality, advertising from a position of dominance it no longer holds instead of doing the hard work of rediscovering its identity.
